# Exodus Chapter 36 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 107–108; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 79–80 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_036_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_036_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_036_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_036_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Surplus of Offerings (vv. 1–7)

**1** And Bezalel [**Betsal'el**] shall work [**ve'asah**], and Oholiab [**ve'Oholiy'av**] and every [**vekhol**] man [**ish**] wise of [**chakham**] heart [**lev**] in whom [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has given [**natan**] wisdom [**chokmah**] and understanding [**utvunah**] in them [**bahemmah**], to know [**lada'at**] how to do [**la'asot**] all [**et-kol**] the work of [**mele'khet**] the service of [**avodat**] the holy place [**haqqodesh**], according to all [**lekhol**] that [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has commanded [**tsivvah**].

**2** And Moses called [**vayyiqra Mosheh**] to [**el**] Bezalel [**Betsal'el**] and to [**ve'el**] Oholiab [**Oholiy'av**] and to [**ve'el**] every [**kol**] man [**ish**] wise of [**chakham**] heart [**lev**] in whose [**asher**] heart [**belibbo**] YHWH [**YHWH**] had given [**natan**] wisdom [**chokmah**] — every one [**kol**] whose [**asher**] heart [**lev**] stirred him [**nesa'o libbo**] to draw near [**liqrevah**] to [**el**] the work [**hammelakhah**] to do it [**la'asotah**].

**3** And they received [**vayyiqechu**] from before [**milllifney**] Moses [**Mosheh**] all [**et-kol**] the contribution [**hatterumah**] which [**asher**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] had brought [**hevi'u**] for the work of [**limle'khet**] the service of [**avodat**] the holy place [**haqqodesh**] to do it [**la'asotah**]. And they [**vehem**] still brought [**hevi'u**] to him [**elav**] a freewill offering [**nedavah**] morning by morning [**babboqer babboqer**].

**4** And all [**vayyavo'u kol**] the wise ones [**hachakhamim**] who were doing [**ha'osim**] all [**et kol**] the work of [**mele'khet**] the holy place [**haqqodesh**] came [**vayyavo'u**], each man [**ish ish**] from his work [**mimmelakhto**] which [**asher**] they [**hemmah**] were doing [**osim**].

**5** And they said [**vayyomeru**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**], saying [**lemor**]: "The people [**ha'am**] are bringing [**marbim**] more [**marbim**] than enough [**lehavi**] — more than [**middey**] the service [**ha'avodah**] requires [**lammela'khah**] — for the work [**lammela'khah**] which [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has commanded [**tsivvah**] to do [**la'asot**] it [**otah**]."

**6** And Moses commanded [**vayetsav Mosheh**], and they caused a proclamation to pass [**vayya'aviru qol**] through the camp [**bammachaneh**], saying [**lemor**]: "Let no [**al**] man [**ish**] or [**ve**] woman [**ishah**] make [**ya'asu**] any more [**od**] work [**melakhah**] for the contribution of [**literumat**] the holy place [**haqqodesh**]." And the people [**ha'am**] were restrained [**vayyikkale**] from bringing [**mehavi**].

**7** And the work [**vehammelakhah**] was sufficient [**dayam**] for them [**hayetah**] for all [**lekhol**] the work [**hammelakhah**] to do it [**la'asot otah**], and more than enough [**vehoter**].

---

## The Curtains of the Tabernacle (vv. 8–13)

**8** And all [**vayyasa'u kol**] the wise ones of [**chakham**] heart [**lev**] among the doers of [**osey**] the work [**hammelakhah**] made [**vayyasa'u**] the Tabernacle [**et-hammishkan**]: ten [**eser**] curtains [**yeri'ot**] of fine twisted linen [**shesh moshezar**] and blue [**utekhelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet of [**vetola'at**] crimson [**shani**], with cherubim [**keruvim**] — the work of [**ma'aseh**] a designer [**choshev**] — he made [**asah**] them [**otam**].

**9** The length of [**orekh**] the one [**hayeri'ah ha'echat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] was twenty-eight [**shemoneh ve'esrim**] cubits [**ba'ammah**], and the width [**verechev**] was four [**arba**] cubits [**ba'ammah**] for the one [**hayeri'ah ha'echat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**]; one [**middah achat**] measure [**middah**] for all [**lekhol**] the curtains [**hayeri'ot**].

**10** And he joined [**vayechabber**] five [**chamesh**] of the curtains [**hayeri'ot**] one [**echat**] to [**el**] another [**echat**], and five [**vechamesh**] curtains [**yeri'ot**] he joined [**chibber**] one [**echat**] to [**el**] another [**echat**].

**11** And he made [**vayya'as**] loops of [**lule'ot**] blue [**tekhelet**] upon [**al**] the edge of [**sefat**] the one [**hayeri'ah ha'echat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] at the end [**miqqatseh**] of the joining [**bammachberet**]; likewise [**ken**] he made [**asah**] on the edge of [**bisefat**] the outermost [**haqqitsonah**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] in the second [**bammachberet hasshenit**] joining [**bammachberet**].

**12** Fifty [**chamishim**] loops [**lula'ot**] he made [**asah**] on the one [**bayeri'ah ha'echat**] curtain [**bayeri'ah**], and fifty [**vachamishim**] loops [**lula'ot**] he made [**asah**] at the end of [**biqtseh**] the curtain [**hayeri'ah**] which [**asher**] was in the second [**bammachberet hasshenit**] joining [**bammachberet**]; the loops [**maqqbilot**] were opposite [**maqqbilot**] one [**hallula'ot**] to [**echat**] another [**el-echat**].

**13** And he made [**vayya'as**] fifty [**chamishim**] clasps of [**qarsey**] gold [**zahav**], and he joined [**vayechabber**] the curtains [**et-hayeri'ot**] one [**echat**] to [**el**] another [**echat**] with the clasps [**baqqerasim**]; and the Tabernacle became [**vayehi**] one [**hammishkan echad**].

---

## The Goat Hair Curtains (vv. 14–19)

**14** And he made [**vayya'as**] curtains of [**yeri'ot**] goats' hair [**izzim**] for a tent [**le'ohel**] over [**al**] the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**]; eleven [**ashtey-esreh**] curtains [**yeri'ot**] he made [**asah**] them [**otam**].

**15** The length of [**orekh**] the one [**hayeri'ah ha'echat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] was thirty [**sheloshim**] cubits [**ba'ammah**], and four [**arba**] cubits [**ammot**] was the width of [**rechev**] the one [**hayeri'ah ha'echat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**]; one [**middah achat**] measure [**middah**] for the eleven [**le'ashtey esreh**] curtains [**yeri'ot**].

**16** And he joined [**vayechabber**] five [**et-chamesh**] of the curtains [**hayeri'ot**] by themselves [**levad**] and six [**ve'et-shesh**] of the curtains [**hayeri'ot**] by themselves [**levad**].

**17** And he made [**vayya'as**] fifty [**chamishim**] loops [**lula'ot**] upon [**al**] the edge of [**sefat**] the outermost [**haqqitsonah**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] in the joining [**bammachberet**], and fifty [**vachamishim**] loops [**lula'ot**] he made [**asah**] upon [**al**] the edge of [**sefat**] the curtain [**hayeri'ah**] of the second [**hasshenit**] joining [**hammachberet**].

**18** And he made [**vayya'as**] fifty [**chamishim**] clasps of [**qarsey**] bronze [**nechoshet**] to join [**lechabber**] the tent [**et-ha'ohel**] to be [**lihyot**] one [**echad**].

**19** And he made [**vayya'as**] a covering [**mikhseh**] for the tent [**la'ohel**] of rams' [**orot eylim**] skins [**orot**] dyed red [**me'oddamim**] and a covering of [**umikh'seh**] tahash [**orot techashim**] skins [**orot**] from above [**milma'elah**].

---

## The Frames of the Tabernacle (vv. 20–30)

**20** And he made [**vayya'as**] the boards [**et-haqqerashim**] for the Tabernacle [**lammishkan**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**], standing upright [**omedim**].

**21** Ten [**eser**] cubits [**ammot**] was the length of [**orekh**] the board [**haqqaresh**], and a cubit [**ammah**] and a half [**vachatsi**] cubit [**ha'ammah**] was the width of [**rechev**] the one [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] board [**haqqeresh**].

**22** Two [**shtey**] tenons [**yadot**] for the one [**laqqeresh ha'echad**] board [**laqqeresh**], joined [**meshullavot**] one [**echat**] to [**el**] another [**echat**]; thus [**ken**] he made [**asah**] for all [**lekhol**] the boards of [**qarshey**] the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**].

**23** And he made [**vayya'as**] the boards [**et-haqqerashim**] for the Tabernacle [**lammishkan**]: twenty [**esrim**] boards [**qeresh**] for the south [**negev**] side [**lifat**], southward [**teymanah**].

**24** And forty [**ve'arba'im**] bases of [**adney**] silver [**khesef**] he made [**asah**] under [**tachat**] the twenty [**esrim**] boards [**haqqeresh**]: two [**shney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the one [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] board [**haqqeresh**] for its two [**lishtey**] tenons [**yadotav**], and two [**ushney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the other [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] board [**haqqeresh**] for its two [**lishtey**] tenons [**yadotav**].

**25** And for the second [**hasshenit**] side [**uletsel'a**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**], for the north [**tsafon**] side [**lifat**], he made [**asah**] twenty [**esrim**] boards [**qeresh**],

**26** and their forty [**ve'arba'im**] bases of [**adneyhem**] silver [**khesef**]: two [**shney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the one [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] board [**haqqeresh**] and two [**ushney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the other [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] board [**haqqeresh**].

**27** And for the rear of [**uleyarketey**] the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**], westward [**yammah**], he made [**asah**] six [**shisha**] boards [**qerashim**].

**28** And two [**ushney**] boards [**qerashim**] he made [**asah**] for the corners of [**limqutso'ot**] the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] at the rear [**bayyarketayim**].

**29** And they were [**vehayu**] twinned [**to'amim**] from below [**millematah**], and together [**yachdav**] they were [**yihyu**] complete [**tammim**] at its top [**el-rosho**], to [**el**] the one [**hattabba'at ha'echat**] ring [**hattabba'at**]; thus [**ken**] he made [**asah**] for both of them [**lishney'hem**], for the two [**lishtey**] corners [**hammiqtso'ot**].

**30** And there were [**vehayu**] eight [**shemonah**] boards [**qerashim**] and their bases of [**ve'adneyhem**] silver [**kesef**] — sixteen [**shishah asar**] bases [**adanim**]: two [**shney**] bases [**adanim**], two bases [**shney adanim**] under [**tachat**] each [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] board [**haqqeresh**].

---

## The Bars (vv. 31–34)

**31** And he made [**vayya'as**] bars of [**berichey**] acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**]: five [**chamishah**] for the boards of [**leqarshey**] the one [**tsel'a**] side [**tsel'a**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**],

**32** and five [**vachamishah**] bars [**berichim**] for the boards of [**leqarshey**] the second [**tsel'a**] side [**tsel'a**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**], and five [**vachamishah**] bars [**berichim**] for the boards of [**leqarshey**] the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] at the rear [**layyarketayim**], westward [**yammah**].

**33** And he made [**vayya'as**] the middle [**habberiach hattikon**] bar [**habberiach**] to pass through [**livroach**] in the midst of [**betokh**] the boards [**haqqerashim**] from end [**min-haqqatseh**] to [**el**] end [**haqqatseh**].

**34** And the boards [**ve'et-haqqerashim**] he overlaid [**tsippah**] with gold [**zahav**], and their rings [**ve'et-tabbeotam**] he made [**asah**] of gold [**zahav**] — holders [**batim**] for the bars [**labberichim**]; and the bars [**ve'et-habberichim**] he overlaid [**tsippah**] with gold [**zahav**].

---

## The Veil and the Screen (vv. 35–38)

**35** And he made [**vayya'as**] the veil [**et-happarokhet**] of blue [**tekhelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet of [**vetola'at**] crimson [**shani**] and fine twisted linen [**veshesh moshezar**]; the work of [**ma'aseh**] a designer [**choshev**] he made it [**asah otah**] — with cherubim [**keruvim**].

**36** And he made [**vayya'as**] for it [**lah**] four [**arba'ah**] pillars of [**ammudey**] acacia [**shittim**], and he overlaid them [**vayetsappem**] with gold [**zahav**]; their hooks [**vaveyhem**] were of gold [**zahav**]; and he cast [**vayyitsoq**] for them [**lahem**] four [**arba'ah**] bases of [**adney**] silver [**khasef**].

**37** And he made [**vayya'as**] a screen [**masakh**] for the entrance of [**lefetach**] the tent [**ha'ohel**] of blue [**tekhelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet of [**vetola'at**] crimson [**shani**] and fine twisted linen [**veshesh moshezar**] — the work of [**ma'aseh**] an embroiderer [**roqem**],

**38** and its five [**ve'et-ammudav chamishah**] pillars [**ammudav**] and their hooks [**vaveyhem**]; and he overlaid [**vetsippah**] their tops [**rasheyhem**] and their bands [**vachashukeyhem**] with gold [**zahav**]; and their five [**vachamishah**] bases [**adneyhem**] were of bronze [**nechoshet**].

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

1. **Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 107–108:** The text of Exodus 36 begins on folio 107 (page 1 image), continuing directly from the Bezalel/Oholiab appointment at the end of chapter 35. No parashah petuchah or setumah marker separates 35:35 from 36:1 in the Leningrad Codex; the narrative flows without break from the appointment of the artisans to their commencement of work, confirming the literary unity of 35:30–36:7 as a single pericope. The text continues through the left column of folio 107 and onto folio 108 (page 2 image). The Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks are fully preserved throughout. The phrase "babboqer babboqer" (morning by morning) in v. 3 carries distinctive cantillation, with the repetition of the noun functioning as a distributive temporal expression. The detailed construction account (vv. 8–38) occupies the bulk of folio 108, with the three-column format maintained. A masorah parva note appears adjacent to the rare Niphal "vayyikkale" (were restrained) in v. 6. The defective and plene spellings throughout the construction specifications are consistent with Tiberian scribal practice.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 79–80:** The Vaticanus Greek text covering Exodus 36 material presents the well-known divergence in chapter and verse ordering between the LXX and the MT for the Tabernacle construction narrative. In the LXX arrangement, the material corresponding to MT Exodus 36:8–38 (the curtains, frames, bars, veil, and screen) appears in a different sequence — much of it corresponds to LXX Exodus 36 (= MT Exodus 26, the original Tabernacle instructions), while the actual construction account is rearranged in LXX Exodus 37. The Vaticanus manuscript on pages 79–80 shows the continuation of the Tabernacle construction material in the three-column uncial scriptio continua format. The chapter heading KS (= 26) is visible on page 79, confirming the LXX numbering. The surplus-offering narrative (MT 36:1–7) finds its LXX parallel in Exodus 36:1–7 of the Greek, where the key verb "ekolusthesan" (they were restrained) in v. 6 corresponds to MT "vayyikkale." The phrase "to ergon hikanan" (the work was sufficient) in v. 7 renders the Hebrew "vehammelakhah hayetah dayam." The construction details in the Vaticanus text on pages 79–80 track the parallel construction commands (LXX ch. 26/MT ch. 26) rather than the fulfillment account, reflecting the LXX's distinctive structural arrangement of the Tabernacle pericope.

### Key Translation Decisions

3. **v. 1 — "ve'asah Betsal'el ve'Oholiy'av" (and Bezalel shall work, and Oholiab):** The waw-consecutive perfect "ve'asah" continues the discourse from 35:35, carrying forward YHWH's command. The verse is syntactically complex: the subject ("Bezalel and Oholiab and every wise-hearted man") governs the infinitive chain "to know how to do all the work." The phrase "natan YHWH chokmah utvunah bahemmah" (YHWH has given wisdom and understanding in them) uses the preposition "be" (in) rather than "le" (to), indicating that the wisdom is placed within the artisans, not merely directed toward them. The LXX renders "kai epoiesen Beseleel kai Eliab" (and Bezalel and Eliab worked), reading the name Oholiab as Eliab — a consistent variant in the LXX tradition.

4. **v. 2 — "kol asher nesa'o libbo liqrevah el hammelakhah" (every one whose heart stirred him to draw near to the work):** The infinitive "liqrevah" (to draw near) is a cultic term — the same root (qarav) used for bringing offerings and approaching sacred space. The artisans do not merely commence work; they "draw near" to it, as a priest draws near to the altar. This sacralizes the act of construction and places the artisans' labor in the same conceptual domain as priestly service.

5. **v. 3 — "babboqer babboqer" (morning by morning):** The distributive repetition of the noun (boqer boqer) indicates continuous, ongoing action — "each and every morning." This is a standard Hebrew idiom for distributive temporal expressions (cf. "yom yom," day by day). The freewill offerings continued to arrive daily, demonstrating sustained willingness. The LXX renders "to proi proi" (each morning).

6. **v. 5 — "marbim ha'am lehavi" (the people are bringing much / more than enough):** The Hiphil participle "marbim" (making much, increasing) modifies the infinitive "lehavi" (to bring) — literally "the people are making-much to bring." This is a remarkable statement: the surplus of generosity exceeds the capacity of the work to absorb it. The phrase "middey ha'avodah" (more than sufficient for the service) uses "middey" (a construct of "day" + min, meaning "enough of" or "more than sufficient") as a comparative marker. The LXX reads "plethunei ho laos pherein" (the people are multiplying in bringing).

7. **v. 6 — "vayyikkale ha'am mehavi" (and the people were restrained from bringing):** The Niphal "vayyikkale" (from kala', to restrain, hold back, shut up) is passive — the people were restrained, not by their own volition but by command. Moses must issue a proclamation to stop the giving. This is one of the most theologically significant verses in the Tabernacle narrative: the only time in Scripture where a collection for a sacred project must be halted because of excess generosity. The verb "vayya'aviru qol" (they caused a voice/proclamation to pass) uses the Hiphil of 'avar (to pass over, cross) with "qol" (voice), a technical term for public proclamation — literally "they made a voice pass through the camp." The LXX reads "ekolusthe ho laos eti prospherein" (the people were prevented from still bringing).

8. **v. 7 — "vehammelakhah hayetah dayam ... vehoter" (and the work was sufficient for them ... and more than enough):** The verse concludes the surplus pericope with a summary statement using two terms: "dayam" (sufficient, enough for them) and "vehoter" (and surplus, remainder). The final word "vehoter" stands alone, an emphatic coda — there was not merely enough but an overflow. This abundance motif contrasts with the wilderness scarcity narratives and anticipates the divine provision theology of the Tabernacle.

9. **vv. 8–13 — The Tabernacle curtains:** The construction of the inner curtains (vv. 8–13) closely mirrors the instructions in Exodus 26:1–6, forming the command-fulfillment pair. The specifications are repeated with near-verbatim fidelity: ten curtains of fine twisted linen with blue, purple, and scarlet, adorned with cherubim (the work of a designer), twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide, joined in sets of five with fifty loops of blue and fifty gold clasps. The repetition is deliberate, not redundant — it demonstrates exact obedience. The phrase "vayehi hammishkan echad" (and the Tabernacle became one) in v. 13 carries deep theological resonance: the diverse components are unified into a single dwelling. The LXX follows the same structural pattern.

10. **vv. 14–19 — The goat hair curtains and coverings:** The outer curtain layer mirrors Exodus 26:7–14. Eleven curtains of goats' hair, thirty cubits by four cubits, joined in sets of five and six, with fifty bronze clasps. The ram-skin covering dyed red and the tahash-skin covering above complete the four-layer tent structure. The term "techashim" (tahash) in v. 19 remains untranslated as the precise identification of this material is uncertain — proposals include dugong, dolphin, fine leather, or a color term. The LXX renders "dermata huakinthina" (hyacinth-colored skins).

11. **vv. 20–30 — The frames and bases:** The frame construction mirrors Exodus 26:15–25. The boards (qerashim) of acacia wood, ten cubits by one and a half cubits, with two tenons (yadot, literally "hands") per board. The forty silver bases for the south wall, forty for the north wall, and sixteen for the rear (west) wall — with the two corner boards "twinned from below" (to'amim millematah) — total ninety-six bases. The term "to'amim" (twinned) in v. 29, from the root ta'am (to be twin, to be double), describes the corner boards as paired or doubled, joined at the bottom and united at the top to a single ring. This structural detail ensures the corner integrity of the Tabernacle frame.

12. **v. 22 — "yadot" (tenons, literally "hands"):** The metaphorical use of "yad" (hand) for the projecting tenons that fit into the silver bases illustrates the Hebrew tendency toward embodied architectural language. Each board has two "hands" that grip its foundation, making the Tabernacle frame a structure that "holds on" to its base.

13. **vv. 31–34 — The bars:** Five bars per side (south, north, west), with the middle bar passing through the center of the boards from end to end. The boards are overlaid with gold, with gold rings serving as "batim" (houses, holders) for the bars, and the bars themselves overlaid with gold. The term "batim" (v. 34), literally "houses," is used architecturally for settings or sockets — the same term used for the gem settings in the breastpiece (28:11). The bars were "housed" in their rings as stones are "housed" in their bezels.

14. **v. 33 — "habberiach hattikon livroach betokh haqqerashim" (the middle bar to pass through in the midst of the boards):** The middle bar (beriach tikon) is distinguished from the other bars by running through the interior of the boards themselves, not merely along their surface. The infinitive "livroach" (to flee, to pass through) shares its root with "beriach" (bar/bolt), both from the root ברח (barach). The bar "flees" through the boards — a kinetic image for a structural brace that traverses the entire wall length.

15. **vv. 35–36 — The veil (parokhet):** The inner veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place is made of the same materials as the inner curtains — blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twisted linen — with cherubim worked by a designer (choshev). It hangs on four gold-overlaid acacia pillars set in four silver bases. The veil (parokhet) functions as a boundary marker between accessible and inaccessible sacred space, the threshold beyond which only the high priest may pass on the Day of Atonement. The LXX renders "to katapetasma" (the curtain/veil), the same term used in the NT for the temple veil torn at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:51).

16. **vv. 37–38 — The screen (masakh):** The entrance screen differs from the inner veil in two respects: it is the work of an embroiderer (roqem) rather than a designer (choshev), and it hangs on five pillars with bronze bases rather than four pillars with silver bases. The distinction between "choshev" (designer — who works cherubim into the fabric) and "roqem" (embroiderer — who works decorative patterns without figural imagery) maintains a graded holiness: the innermost veil, closest to YHWH's presence, bears cherubim; the outer screen does not. The material gradation (silver bases for the veil, bronze for the screen) reinforces the same hierarchical principle — proximity to YHWH's presence corresponds to increasing material purity.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

17. **The Overflow of Generosity (vv. 1–7):** The opening pericope presents a unique scene in biblical narrative: the cessation of a sacred collection due to surplus. The people's willingness, kindled in chapter 35, has produced an abundance that exceeds the project's requirements. The theological function of this surplus is multifaceted. First, it validates the "willing heart" principle of 35:5 — voluntary giving, when properly motivated, produces not scarcity but overflow. Second, it reverses the golden calf economy: in chapter 32, the people stripped off their gold for an idol; here they give so lavishly for YHWH's dwelling that they must be restrained. Third, the surplus anticipates the eschatological abundance theme — when God's people give willingly, there is "vehoter" (more than enough), a single-word theological statement that overflows the sentence itself.

18. **Command-Fulfillment Precision (vv. 8–38):** The construction narrative of vv. 8–38 corresponds almost verbatim to the instructions of Exodus 26. This near-exact repetition constitutes the heart of the Priestly writer's theological method: obedience is demonstrated by correspondence. Every dimension, material, color, and structural detail echoes the original command, forming a literary diptych where the left panel (ch. 26) displays YHWH's word and the right panel (ch. 36) displays Israel's obedient work. The repetition is liturgical in character — like a responsive reading where the congregation repeats the words of the officiant, confirming reception and compliance.

19. **The Unity of the Tabernacle ("vayehi hammishkan echad," v. 13):** The clause "and the Tabernacle became one" is more than an architectural observation. The diverse materials (linen, dyed wool, gold, silver, bronze, acacia, animal skins) and the diverse workers (Bezalel, Oholiab, wise-hearted men and women) are unified into a single dwelling. The echad (one) of the Tabernacle mirrors the echad of the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4) — YHWH who is one dwells in a Tabernacle that is one. The physical unity of the structure embodies the theological unity of its divine Inhabitant.

20. **Graded Holiness in Materials and Craftsmanship:** The chapter preserves the Priestly system of graduated sanctity. The inner curtains are fine linen with cherubim (choshev work); the outer curtains are goat hair (no decoration). The inner veil has cherubim on silver bases; the entrance screen has embroidery on bronze bases. The gradation runs: gold > silver > bronze; cherubim > embroidery > plain; fine linen > goat hair > animal skins. Each material transition marks a step away from the innermost sanctum. This spatial theology of holiness — increasing purity and costliness as one moves toward YHWH's presence — is the architectural expression of the Priestly understanding that proximity to the Holy requires corresponding elevation of material and craft.

21. **Formal Equivalence Methodology:** This translation maintains strict word-order fidelity to the Hebrew, preserves all waw-consecutive and waw-copulative conjunctions, retains the repetitive construction specifications without condensation or paraphrase, and provides transliterated glosses for each Hebrew term. The near-verbatim correspondence between this chapter and Exodus 26 is preserved in translation to maintain the command-fulfillment literary architecture. YHWH is transliterated throughout. "Sons of Israel" is maintained. Standard name forms (Bezalel, Oholiab) are used. Technical terms (parokhet, choshev, roqem, tahash) are translated with consistent English equivalents across chapters.
